coredata.dc:description: Establishment of a rapid, sensitive, visual, accurate and low-cost fluorescence detection system to detect multiple targets was of great significance in food safety evaluation, ecological environment monitoring and human health monitoring. In this work, a smartphone-assisted down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor was proposed based on metal-organic framework (NH2-MIL-101(Fe)) and CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) for visual detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) and L-penicillamine (L-PA), in which NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was used as the reference signal and CdTe QDs was used as the response signal. The down-conversion fluorescence system at excitation wavelength of 300 nm (ex: 330 nm) was used to detect Hg2+ and L-PA, in which the detection limit of Hg2+ was 0.053 nM with the fluorescence color changed from green to blue, and the detection limit of L-PA was 1.10 nM with the fluorescence color changed from blue to green. Meanwhile, the up-conversion fluorescence system at excitation wavelength of 700 nm (ex: 700 nm) was used to detect Hg2+ and L-PA. The detection limits of Hg2+ and L-PA were 0.11 nM and 2.93 nM, respectively. The detection of Hg2+ and L-PA were also carried out based on the color extraction RGB values identified by the smartphone with a detection limit of 0.091 nM for Hg2+ and 8.97 nM for L-PA. In addition, the concentrations of Hg2+ and L-PA were evaluated by three-dimensional dynamic analysis in complex environments. The smartphone-assisted down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor system provides a new strategy for detection Hg2+ and L-PA in food safety evaluation, environmental monitoring and human health monitoring.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-assisted down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor for visual detection of mercury ions and l-penicillamine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Establishment of a rapid, sensitive, visual, accurate and low-cost fluorescence detection system to detect multiple targets was of great significance in food safety evaluation, ecological environment monitoring and human health monitoring. In this work, a smartphone-assisted down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor was proposed based on metal-organic framework (NH2-MIL-101(Fe)) and CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) for visual detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) and L-penicillamine (L-PA), in which NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was used as the reference signal and CdTe QDs was used as the response signal. The down-conversion fluorescence system at excitation wavelength of 300 nm (ex: 330 nm) was used to detect Hg2+ and L-PA, in which the detection limit of Hg2+ was 0.053 nM with the fluorescence color changed from green to blue, and the detection limit of L-PA was 1.10 nM with the fluorescence color changed from blue to green. Meanwhile, the up-conversion fluorescence system at excitation wavelength of 700 nm (ex: 700 nm) was used to detect Hg2+ and L-PA. The detection limits of Hg2+ and L-PA were 0.11 nM and 2.93 nM, respectively. The detection of Hg2+ and L-PA were also carried out based on the color extraction RGB values identified by the smartphone with a detection limit of 0.091 nM for Hg2+ and 8.97 nM for L-PA. In addition, the concentrations of Hg2+ and L-PA were evaluated by three-dimensional dynamic analysis in complex environments. The smartphone-assisted down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor system provides a new strategy for detection Hg2+ and L-PA in food safety evaluation, environmental monitoring and human health monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industry, toxic heavy metal ions and chiral drugs can cause serious environmental pollution and human health problems (Huang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022). Mercury ion (Hg2+) is a high toxic heavy metal ion, which has serious toxic effects on the human brain, kidneys, and heart, even leading to cancer (Liu et al., 2022a, 2022b; Yan et al., 2016). l-Penicillamine (L-PA) is the metabolite of penicillin antibiotics, which are not used in clinics because of their unique toxicological properties. L-PA may cause impaired kidneys, damage to bone tissue, impaired smell, and cancer (Gigante et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021). Hg2+ and L-PA, as toxic substances, may be accumulated through food chain, which will seriously damage human health and the ecosystem (Lu et al., 2022b; Wu et al., 2022). Analysis of two related indicators (Hg2+, L-PA) can effectively reduce environmental pollution and improve the accuracy of cancer diagnosis. Currently, numbers of analytical methods have been reported for the determination of Hg2+ and L-PA, including electrochemical method, colorimetric analysis, flow injection, high performance liquid chromatography, and fluorescence spectroscopy (Wang et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2022b; Lian et al., 2022; Rajabi et al., 2015; Wangfuengkanagul and Chailapakul, 2002; Zhu et al., 2017; Bhushan and Dubey, 2011; Wang et al., 2022a, 2022b; Durán et al., 2017). Although these techniques have obtained positive results, time-consuming, complex sample pretreatment, and non-portability have restricted their widespread availability. Recently, fluorescence sensing has attracted attention in the trace detection due to its advantages of rapidity, accuracy, low cost, and visualization (Fu et al., 2022a).

CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) and metal-organic framework (NH2-MIL-101(Fe)) were commonly used fluorescence materials with high quantum efficiency, excitation spectrum width and other excellent photophysical properties, which have been widely used in food safety, environmental monitoring and human health monitoring (Chen et al., 2022; Huang and Tong, 2022; Tang et al., 2022a; Song et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019). To date, some studies developed single wavelength fluorescence intensity-based CdTe QDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe) to detect toxic substances in complex environments (Karthik et al., 2017; Qi et al., 2017). However, single fluorescence sensors were susceptible to interference by non-analytical factors such as fluorescence intensity of the background, photobleaching of fluorophores, and light scattering from the sample, which reduce the accuracy of the results (Wu et al., 2022). To overcome these effects, a self-correcting down-ratiometric fluorescence sensor has been developed, which has been widely used in various fields (Tang et al., 2022b). However, the down-ratiometric fluorescence sensor was susceptible to background signals, which resulted in the lower sensitivity and accuracy. Interestingly, the up-conversion fluorescence material just reduces this interference (Fu et al., 2022b), which was attributed to the unique anti-Stokes shift properties of up-conversion materials converting low light energy into high-energy radiation through a nonlinear optical process (Fu et al., 2022c). In recent years, outstanding image processing functions and powerful data processing capabilities have led to the rapid development of smartphones in the field of analytics (Aydindogan et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020). By using color recognition software, the fluorescence colors are transformed into RGB (R: red channel, G: green channel, B: blue channel) values for quantitative analysis (Wei et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2021). Wang et al. reported an inkjet print paper-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor coupled with a smartphone for quantitative analysis of lead ions by recognizing color RGB values (Wang et al., 2019). Tang et al. conducted a smartphone sensing platform based on the single fluorescence of black wolfberry carbon dots for the real-time detection of silver ions (Tang et al., 2022a, 2022b, 2022c). This approach simplifies the detection steps, which promotes the development of fluorescence sensors. To our knowledge, there is no report on the detection of Hg2+ and L-PA based on the same smartphone fluorescence sensor.

Herein, a smartphone-assisted down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor for visual detection of Hg2+ and L-PA was proposed. As depicted in Scheme 1A, NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was synthesized with a solvothermal technique. As indicated in Scheme 1B, CdTe QDs with 3-mercaptopropionic acid as a stabilizer was prepared with a solvothermal approach. As shown in Scheme 1C, the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed by adjusting the volume ratio between NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and CdTe QDs, in which NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was used as the reference signal (433 nm) and CdTe QDs was used as the response signal (544 nm). When Hg2+ was added to the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor system, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs at 554 nm decreased gradually, and the color of the fluorescence sensor changed from green to blue under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. Interestingly, when L-PA was added to the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor in the presence of Hg2+ (NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/CdTe QDs/Hg2+), the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs at 554 nm recovered gradually, and the fluorescence color of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor changed from blue to green under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. In addition, the fluorescence color change of the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor was recognized by a smartphone, which was converted to RGB values for analysis. The smartphone-assisted down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor provided a new strategy for detection of Hg2+ and L-PA in water samples, meat samples, and human urine samples with satisfactory results.

2-Aminoterephthalic acid (2-NH2-1,4-BDC), l-penicillamine (L-PA), ethanol, acetic acid (HAc), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (99.0 %, MPA), cadmium chloride hemi (pentahydrate) (99.0 %, CdCl2·2.5H2O), FeCl3·6H2O, and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HgCl2, NiCl2, ZnCl2, CoCl2, AlCl3, NaOH, KCl, NaCl, CuCl2, NH4Cl, Pb(NO3)2, AgNO3, tellurium powder (99.99 %), sodium borohydride (98.0 %, NaBH4), l-phenylalanine(L-Phe), l-aspartic acid (L-Asp), l-valine (L-Val), glutathione (GSH), l-histidine (L-His), l-cysteine (L-Cys), l-tyrosine (L-Tyr), methanol, acetone, sodium diethyldithiocarbamatre (DDTC), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was utilized in the entire study and the reagents used were all of the analytical purity.

The fluorescence signals were recorded by an F-7000 spectrofluorometer (Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Shimadzu-1780 spectrophotometer (Japan). Fourier transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (USA). Dissolution of the material was performed in an ultrasonic bath (China). The morphology and structure of the material were characterized by JEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the material were obtained by a JSM-7800F microscope (Japan). The centrifugation operation was performed on a Neofuge 15 high-speed centrifuge (China).

According to the previous literature with minor modifications (Li et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022a, 2022b), NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was prepared by a solvothermal method using 2-NH2-1,4-BDC as an organic ligand and Fe3+ as metal nodes. Firstly, 0.68 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.45 g of 2-NH2-1,4-BDC were quickly dissolved in 25 mL of DMF containing 2.5 mL of acetic acid. Next, the above mixture was reacted in a 50 mL autoclave at 120 °C for 12 h. Collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, the prepared material was washed with DMF/acetone (9:1, v/v) for three times, and then dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

The CdTe QDs were synthesized according to previous method with some modifications (Ran et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020a; Yang et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2022a; Han et al., 2020). Firstly, 0.191 g of tellurium powder and 0.152 g of NaBH4 were quickly dissolved in 4.5 mL of ultrapure water under nitrogen atmosphere with magnetic stirring (100 rpm) for 50 min at room temperature. Next, 0.648 g of CdCl2·2.5 H2O and 638 μL of MPA were quickly dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water, in which the solution pH was adjusted to 6 with 0.1 mol/ L of NaOH solution. Then, the above solutions were mixed for 30 min under nitrogen atmosphere. After that, the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel and reacted at 180 °C for 25 min. After cooling, the CdTe QDs were precipitated with ethanol, purified by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 3 min and washed with ethanol for three times. After that, the purified CdTe QDs solution was freeze-dried for 12 h to obtain CdTe QDs solid powder. Finally, 2.5 mg of CdTe QDs solid powder re-dispersed in 10 ml of ultrapure water to obtain a 0.25 mg/mL CdTe QDs stock solution was preserved in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Down/up conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and L-PA
ce:section.ce:para: The down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and L-PA was carried out in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7). For determination of Hg2+: Firstly, 20 μL of 0.14 mg/mL NH2-MIL-101(Fe), 75 μL of 0.25 mg/mL CdTe QDs and 905 μL of PBS (pH = 7) were mixed to obtain a ratiometric fluorescence sensor. Then, Hg2+ solution with different concentrations were added into the ratiometric fluorescence sensor, which was reacted for 2 min at 35 °C. Finally, the fluorescence spectra in the wavelength range of 400–600 nm were recorded respectively under the excitation wavelength of 330 nm and 700 nm. For detection of L-PA: Firstly, 100 μL of L-PA solution with different concentrations and 100 μL of 1000 nM Hg2+ solution was mixed at room temperature for 10 min to obtain solution A. Meanwhile, 20 μL of 0.14 mg/mL NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and 75 μL of 0.25 mg/mL CdTe QDs were mixed with 905 μL of PBS (pH = 7) to obtain solution B. Then, Solution A and solution B were mixed and reacted at 35 °C for 2 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded under the same conditions as that of Hg2+ detection. The determination conditions of down-conversion ratiometric fluorescence were excitation wavelength of 330 nm, excitation and emission slit width of 5 nm and photomultiplier tube voltage of 530 V. Secondly, the determination conditions of up-conversion ratiometric fluorescence were excitation wavelength of 700 nm, excitation and emission slit width of 10 nm and photomultiplier tube voltage of 650 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted detection of Hg2+ and L-PA
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, Hg2+ solution with different concentrations were dropped into the mixing solution of NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/CdTe (1:3.5, v/v) and diluted to 1 mL with PBS (pH = 7). After kept at 35 °C for 2 min, the mixture solution was transferred to a 3D dark box, in which the color changes of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor were photographed using a smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp irradiated. Next, the RGB values of the fluorescence sensor color change were extracted by a smartphone. Finally, the ratio of G and B channels, as well as the relationship with Hg2+ concentration, were used to achieve quantitative detection. Moreover, several image-capture points were chosen for testing to achieve an average value of G/B or B/G. Similarly, L-PA solutions with different concentrations were added into fluorescence sensor mixture solutions in the presence of Hg2+, and the following steps were the same as for Hg2+ detection by the smartphone.

Water samples were obtained from Fengyu Lake of Jishou University (Hunan, China). Fish and pork were purchased at the supermarket at Jishou University (Hunan, China). Human urine samples were obtained from adult male volunteers. All water samples were pretreated by centrifugation (7000 rpm, 10 min) and 0.22 μM filter membrane to remove the large suspended matter for fluorescence detection (Cai et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2022c). Meat samples were prepared according to reported literature with some modifications (Chen et al., 2022; Fu et al., 2022b). Firstly, 2.5 g of meat samples were ruptured by a high speed blender and then dispersed in a mixture solution of hydroxylamine hydrochloride (20 %, 0.75 mL) and ammonium acetate solution (pH = 4.5, 1.75 mL) with magnetic stirring for 24 h at room temperature. Next, the meat samples were cleaned with PBS (pH = 7) by a high-speed centrifuge. Finally, the supernatant was collected for fluorescence detection. According to a previous method (Zhang et al., 2021), 100 ml of human urine sample was diluted 5 times with PBS (pH = 7) for fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was prepared by a solvothermal method with 2-NH2-1, 4-BDC as organic ligand and Fe3+ as metal nodes with minor modifications. The successful synthesis of NH2-MIL-101-(Fe) was confirmed by several characterization techniques. As shown in Fig. 1A, the characteristic peaks of XRD spectrum of the prepared NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was identical with those of the simulated MIL-101, which was in agreement with that of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) reported in previous literature (Wang et al., 2022a, 2022b), indicating that the NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was successfully synthesized. As shown in Fig. 1B, the SEM image of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) is almond-shaped with a diameter of 10 μm, which is similar as the reported literature (Liu et al., 2021). The results of Fig. 1C–G indicated that the elemental mapping was presented the uniform distribution of C, O, Fe and N elements in the material. The chemical structures of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and 1,4-BDC-NH2 were also analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. As shown in Fig. 1H, 1,4-BDC-NH2(red curve)had a characteristic peak at 1684 cm−1, which was ascribed to the stretching vibration of carboxyl. Two characteristic peaks at 3385 cm−1 and 3499 cm−1 were assigned to the characteristic symmetrical and antisymmetric stretching vibration of NH. NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (blue curve) possessed two characteristic peaks at 3385 cm−1 and 3499 cm−1 assigning to the symmetrical and antisymmetric stretching vibration of NH and a characteristic peak at 1632 cm−1, which was ascribed to the stretching vibration of carboxyl. In addition, a weak signal peak at 567 cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of the FeO bond. All the data indicated that the NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was successfully synthesized.

The CdTe QDs were prepared by a solvothermal method using MPA as stabilizer with some modifications according to the reported method (Ran et al., 2019). The chemical groups of CdTe QDs were characterized by FT-IR, and the results were shown in Fig. 1H. The characteristic peaks at 1627 cm−1 and 3487 cm−1 should be assigned to the stretching vibration of the carboxyl group and the tensile vibration of OH (black curve), which were similar to previous report (Li et al., 2022a, 2022b), indicating the CdTe QDs was synthesized successfully. The morphology of CdTe QDs was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1I and J, the CdTe QDs exhibited the uniform spherical shape distribution with an average size of 4.9 nm. HRTEM images of the CdTe QDs presented that the lattice spacing was 0.238 nm. All the results indicated that the CdTe QDs was synthesized successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of Hg2+ and L-PA detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions for detection of Hg2+ and L-PA were optimized, including the volume ratio of fluorescence source, excitation wavelength, pH, response time, and temperature. Firstly, the volume ratio of the fluorescence sources of the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor was optimized. A series of fluorescence sensor was constructed with the different volume ratios of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and CdTe QDs (1:2, 1:3.5, and 1:5) in detail. When Hg2+ solution with different concentrations were added to the three fluorescence sensors mentioned above, the fluorescence intensity of the sensors were illustrated in Fig. S1A–S1C and the corresponding color changes of the sensors were shown in Fig. S1D. When Hg2+ was added to the fluorescence sensor with a fluorescence source volume ratio of 1:3.5, the color of the fluorescence sensor changed from green to cyan and then to blue under the UV lamp. However, when Hg2+ was added to the fluorescence sensor with a fluorescence source volume ratio was 1:2, the color of the fluorescence sensor changed from cyan to blue. And when Hg2+ was added to the fluorescence sensor with a fluorescence source the volume ratio was 1:5, the color of the fluorescence sensor changed from green to cyan. These results showed that the color change of the fluorescence sensor after the addition of Hg2+ was insignificant when the fluorescence source ration was greater or less than 1:3.5. Therefore, a fluorescence sensor with a fluorescence source volume ratio of 1:3.5 was chosen as the optimal condition for further experiments.

In the excitation wavelength range of 310 nm–370 nm, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) increased firstly and then decreased. The best emission peak at a wavelength of 330 nm (shown in Fig. S1E) was obtained. Meanwhile, the up-conversion fluorescence spectra of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) showed that the up-conversion fluorescence intensity of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) increased firstly and then decreased in the excitation wavelength range of 690 nm–740 nm. The best emission peak was found at the excitation wavelength of 700 nm (shown in Fig. S1F). As depicted in Fig. S1G, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs increased firstly and then decreased with the increase of excitation wavelength in the range of 310 nm–370 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence sensor before and after the addition of Hg2+ was shown in Fig. S1H to vary in the excitation wavelength range of 310 nm–370 nm. Since the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence sensor has the largest variation value at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm (∆Fmax), 330 nm was selected as the best excitation wavelength. For the up-conversion fluorescence characteristics of CdTe QDs, the fluorescence spectra in the near-infrared excitation wavelength range of 690 nm–740 nm were shown in Fig. S1I, and the results indicated that the CdTe QDs had the best emission peak at the excitation wavelength of 700 nm. Fig. S1J depicts the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence sensor before and after the addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence sensor varied in the excitation wavelength range of 690 nm–740 nm, with the largest variation value occurring at 700 nm (∆Fmax). Meanwhile, the down-conversion fluorescence spectra of ratiometric fluorescence sensor was recorded with the excitation of wavelengths ranged from 310 nm to 370 nm and that of the up-conversion fluorescence spectra ranged from 690 nm to 740 nm. The results showed the fluorescence sensor's down-conversion has the strongest emission peaks at 433 nm and 544 nm under excited at 330 nm and 700 nm (shown in Fig. S1K and S1L), respectively. Thus, the excitation wavelength of 330 nm for down-conversion and 700 nm for up-conversion were selected as the optimal excitation wavelength in future studies.

To maximize the fluorescence intensity, pH value of determination system was optimized. As shown in Fig. S1M, when the down-conversion ratiometric fluorescence sensor was added to PBS with different pH values ranged from 5.0 to 10.0, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the down-conversion ratiometric fluorescence sensor decreased firstly and then increased. When the fluorescence sensing system containing with Hg2+ was diluted by PBS (pH = 5.0–10.0), the fluorescence intensity ratio of the fluorescence sensor increased firstly and then decreased, which was attributed to the fact that the CdTe QDs can protonate under acid conditions, resulting in extremely low fluorescence intensity (Ghasemi et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2022a, 2022b). The change of the fluorescence intensity ratio of the fluorescence sensing system with and without Hg2+ reached a maximum at solution pH 7.0, indicating that pH 7.0 was optimal pH value. The effects of different pH values on the up-conversion fluorescence characteristics of the fluorescence sensor were shown in Fig. S1N, indicating that the change of fluorescence intensity ratio before and after adding Hg2+ at different pH was basically same as the change of down-conversion fluorescence characteristics of the fluorescence sensor. Thus, pH 7.0 was selected as the optimal pH value for further experiments.

Fig. S1O and S1P show the effects of temperature range of 10–40 °C on the down/up-conversion fluorescence intensity ratio of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F434/F554) of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was hardly affected by the temperature changes in the range of 10–40 °C. However, when Hg2+ was added to the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor, the fluorescence intensity ratio was firstly enhanced and then decreased in the temperature range of 10–40 °C. At 35 °C, the down/up-conversion fluorescent intensity ratio (F434/F554) reached a maximum, indicating that the quenching of CdTe QDs by Hg2+ was best at 35 °C. Thus, 35 °C was chosen as the best temperature for further experiments.

The effect of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F554) with response time was shown in Fig. S1Q and S1R. When Hg2+ was added to the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F554) of the down/up conversion ratiometric fluorescence gradually increased and both remained stable after 2 min. Thus, 2 min was selected as the optimal reaction time.

For the detection of L-PA, the optimization of the response time was also discussed. As shown in Fig. S1S and S1T, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F554) of the down/up-conversion ratiometric fluorescence sensor was gradually stable after incubated for 10 min by adding different concentrations of L-PA in the presence of Hg2+. Thus, 10 min was chosen as the best response time for the detection of L-PA. In addition, the recovery of the sensor fluorescence intensity by L-PA was studied in the presence of different volumes of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. S1U–S1Z, L-PA had the best response range when the volume of Hg2+ was 100 μL with the concentration of 1000 nM. Thus, 100 μL of 1000 nM Hg2+ was chosen as the best condition for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detection Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor for analysis of Hg2+ was studied under optimum experimental conditions. Fig. 2A and B show that the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs will be quenched gradually, while the fluorescence intensity of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) emitting blue light remained stable with the increase of Hg2+ concentration. As shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F554) of the down-conversion ratiometric fluorescence sensor has a good linear relationship with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.12 nM–2.0 nM and 2.0 nM–1222 nM. The linear regression equation of the low concentration (0.12 nM–2.0 nM) was y = 0.2171x + 0.846 with a coefficient (R2) of 0.994 and that of the high concentration (2.0 nM–1222 nM) was y = 0.0038x + 1.063 with a coefficient (R2) of 0.991. The detection limit was calculated as 0.053 nM (LOD = 3σ/S, σ is the standard deviation of the background (n = 10), S is the slope of the calibration curve). The fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F554) of the up-conversion ratiometric fluorescence probe was linear with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.12 nM–2.0 nM and 2.0 nM–1222 nM, respectively. The linear regression equations were y = 0.1998x + 0.689 with a coefficient (R2) of 0.990 and y = 0.00236x + 1.1486 with a coefficient (R2) of 0.996, respectively. The detection limit was calculated as 0.105 nM (shown in Fig. 2D), which is higher than that of down-conversion ratiometric fluorescence detection system. The reason can be attributed to the fact that the up-conversion ratio fluorescence is excited using a near-infrared light source at 700 nm (Huynh et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 2E, the fluorescence emission peaks of the up-conversion at 433 nm and 544 nm was lower than those of the down-conversion (Huynh et al., 2021). Interestingly, both the detection limits of down-conversion or up-conversion fluorescence system are lower than the permissible levels of Hg2+ in drinking water specified by WHO (30 nM) and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (10 nM) (Şahin et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detection L-PA
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that L-PA as a chelating agent contains sulfhydryl, carboxyl groups and amino functional groups, which resulted in L-PA having a strong affinity for Hg2+. According to the previous reports (Huang et al., 2018; Liang et al., 2019; Pawar et al., 2016; Yuan et al., 2016), the affinity between L-PA and Hg2+ was greater than the force between Hg2+ and CdTe QDs. Thus, the fluorescence color can be restored by adding L-PA to the fluorescence sensor system with Hg2+. In the work, L-PA was detected using a down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor in the presence of Hg2+ (NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/CdTe QDs/Hg2+), in which the volume ratio of NH2-MIL-101(Fe), CdTe QDs and Hg2+ was 1:3.5:5. For down-conversion ratiometric fluorescence detection of L-PA, as shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence color of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor in the presence of Hg2+ (NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/CdTe QDs/Hg2+) gradually recovered from blue to light green with the increasing L-PA concentration. The down-conversion fluorescence recovering degree of NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/CdTe QDs/Hg2+ were linear with L-PA concentration in the range of 10.0 nM–410.0 nM and 410.0 nM–3410.0 nM, respectively. The linear relationship regression equations were y = 7.38–0.01033x with a coefficient (R2) of 0.992 and y = 3.420–0.00069x with a coefficient of 0.998. The detection limit of L-PA was calculated as 1.10 nM (shown in Fig. 3B). Fig. 3C and D showed that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F554) of up-conversion fluorescence gradually decreased with the increase of L-PA concentration in the range of 10.0 nM–410.0 nM and 410.0 nM–4410.0 nM. The linear regression equations were y = 5.943–0.00831x with a coefficient (R2) of 0.993 and y = 2.642–0.00024x with a coefficient (R2) of 0.996, respectively. The detection limit was calculated as 2.93 nM (shown in Fig. 3E and 3F). The down/up-conversion fluorescence sensor has a lower detection limit compared to previous methods (Zhao et al., 2020; Ngamdee et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2016), indicating that this sensor was highly sensitive for the determination of L-PA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted analysis of Hg2+ and L-PA
ce:section.ce:para: When the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was used to detect Hg2+ or L-PA, the color of the fluorescence sensor was changed from green to blue or blue to green under 365 nm UV lamp. However, since it is difficult to observe subtle color changes with naked eyes, directly determine Hg2+ and L-PA content based on the color changes of the solution cannot be achieved (Zhou et al., 2016; Li et al., 2020). To solve this problem, the powerful image processing power of smartphones is used to convert fluorescence colors into digital information RGB values, improving the accuracy of experiments (Yang et al., 2021). In this paper, a portable platform consisting of a 3D dark box, a cuvette tank, a 365 nm UV lamp and a smartphone was home-made to achieve real-time/in situ detection of Hg2+ and L-PA. As shown in Fig. 4A, the color change of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was identified by taking pictures in a portable 3D dark box using a smartphone to extract the RGB value of the color change for rapid analysis of the Hg2+and L-PA. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor showed a color changed from green to blue with the increasing concentration of Hg2+ under 365 nm UV lamp, and the color recognition using smartphone utility software can facilitate the quantitative analysis of Hg2+. Since there is a certain chelating force between L-PA and Hg2+, the fluorescence sensor in the presence of Hg2+ showed a color change from blue to cyan by using 365 nm UV lamp, and the color can be extracted by the same smartphone application software, which can facilitate the quantitative analysis of L-PA. As shown in Fig. 4B and C, the fluorescence color of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor changes from green to blue with the addition of Hg2+ in the concentration range of 0.12 nM–1222 nM, and the RGB values showed the corresponding change. In the specific fluorescence sensor containing Hg2+ with the addition of L-PA, the color of the fluorescence sensor changed from blue to cyan in the L-PA concentration range of 10 nM–4410 nM, and the RGB values also showed the corresponding change. The chromaticity diagram (CIE 1931) color diagram showed that the fluorescence color changed from green to blue with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, which was consistent with the above phenomenon (shown in Fig. 4D). The CIE 1931 color diagram showed that the fluorescence color changed from blue to green with the increase of L-PA concentration, which was consistent with the above phenomenon (shown in Fig. 4E). Fig. 4F and G indicated that the values of blue channel/green channel (B/G) had a linear relationship between Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.12 nM–0.92 nM and 0.92 nM–1222 nM and RGB values were y = 0.4134x + 0.4142 with a correlation coefficient of (R2) 0.990 and y = 0.0017x + 0.7263 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.994, respectively. The detection limit was calculated as 0.091 nM. For the detection of L-PA, the color of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor containing Hg2+ changed from blue to cyan color in the concentration range of 10 nM–360 nM and 360 nM–4410 nM, and the RGB values showed a corresponding change. The values of green channel/blue channel (G/B) had two linear equation with L-PA concentration for the low concentration range was y = 0.00225x − 0.0172 with a correlation coefficient of 0.991 and for the high concentration range was y = 0.00021x + 0.7199 with a correlation coefficient of 0.991. The limit of detection was calculated as 8.97 nM. All results proved that combining smartphones with fluorescence technology allows simple and fast analysis of Hg2+ and L-PA.

Considering the complications of the environment, the fluorescence response of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor toward the mixture of Hg2+ and L-PA with different concentrations was studied by three-dimension dynamic analysis (Xu et al., 2001). As shown in Fig. S2A, when the concentration of Hg2+ was greater than that of L-PA, the ratio of fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence sensor increased. On the contrary, when the concentration of L-PA in the environment was greater than that of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity changed insignificantly. The results showed that when the amount of Hg2+ ions in the environment is equal to the amount of L-PA, Hg2+ is completely chelated by L-PA, in which the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was same as that of without the addition of Hg2+ and L-PA. However, when the amount of Hg2+ was greater than that of L-PA in the complex environment, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the fluorescence sensor gradually increased with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, which was consistent with the fluorescence sensor detecting Hg2+ alone, indicating the unchelated Hg2+ plays a role. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor remained essentially unchanged in a complex environment when the amount of L-PA was greater than the amount of Hg2+, which was attributed to the ability of L-PA to chelate all Hg2+. When the amount of Hg2+ in the complex environment is greater than the amount of L-PA, Hg2+ plays a dominant role in harming the ecosystem and human health. On the contrary, when the amount of L-PA in the complex environment was greater than that of Hg2+, L-PA plays a dominant role in harming the ecological environment and human health. Thus, the levels of Hg2+ and L-PA in different environments can be simply predicted and quantitative analysis by 3D map.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Up-conversion optical properties of CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was a reference signal and CdTe QDs was a response signal, the up-conversion fluorescence characteristic of CdTe QDs was discussed. To date, several studies have confirmed the existence of up-conversion fluorescence in semiconductor quantum dots, and auger recombination and two-photon absorption have been proposed (Joly et al., 2005). As Auger recombination process shown in Fig. S3A, the energy is transferred from one excited electron-hole coupled recombination to another excited electron-hole and forming a highly exciting carrier. Then, the carrier can recombine at a higher energy. Auger processes occur frequently in heterogeneous structures, which require incident photon energies above the band gap to produce the initial electron-hole pair. However, the incident photons from CdTe quantum dots cannot excite electron-hole pairs or other carriers. Therefore, Auger processes cannot explain the mechanism of CdTe up-conversion (Zhang et al., 2010). As shown in Fig. S3B and S3C, there are two ways to induce two-photon absorption. The first one is through an intermediate state within the band gap, which can be called two-step two-photon absorption (TSTPA) and the second one is through a virtual intermediate state (TPA). The up-conversion fluorescence of CdTe QDs was measured by laser diode and F-7000 NIR fluorometer. The faint green fluorescence could be observed in the F-7000 NIR fluorometer, while no fluorescence was presented by the laser diode. Thus, the luminescence mechanism of the conversion on CdTe quantum dots in this experiment belongs to the virtual intermediate state two-photon excitation (Ananthakumar et al., 2016; Joly et al., 2005; Zhang et al., 2010). As shown in Fig. S3D, the emission spectra of CdTe quantum dots excited by visible light at 330 nm and near-infrared laser at 700 nm were similar, indicated that the emission of CdTe quantum dots might come from the same light source (Zhang et al., 2010).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence detection of Hg2+and L-PA
ce:section.ce:para: The response mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescence toward Hg2+ and L-PA was studied by fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–visible absorption spectroscopy. Fig. S4 A shows that the fluorescence intensity of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) at 433 nm change slightly with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, while the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots at 554 nm was gradually quenched with a certain degree of red shift, which suggested the combination of Hg2+ with the sulfydryl of MPA to form HgS particles on CdTe QDs surface (Chen et al., 2019). Fig. S4B and S4C show the UV absorption spectra, emission wavelength and excitation wavelength of different fluorescence sources. Since the UV absorption peak of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) does not overlap with the fluorescence emission peak of CdTe QDs, there was no interference between the two fluorescence sources (Lu et al., 2022b). The UV–Vis spectra of Hg2+, L-PA and Hg2+ + L-PA system were shown in Fig. S4D. Compared with the UV–Vis spectra of Hg2+, L-PA, a new peak appeared at 215 nm in the mixed system of Hg2+ + L-PA, which indicated the formation of Hg2+-L-PA complex (Chen et al., 2019). Therefore, when Hg2+ and L-PA were mixed, Hg2+ can be prevented from binding to sulfhydryl groups on the surface of CdTe QDs, reducing the burst of quantum dots, which explains the ability of L-PA to restore the fluorescence of the up-and-down conversion ratio fluorescence sensing system. As shown in Fig. S4 E, when Hg2+ was added to the ratiometric fluorescence sensor, the UV–Vis absorption spectra did not deviate, indicating that the detection mechanism of Hg2+ was a dynamic process (He et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2020b). The absorption peak was almost unchanged when the mixture of Hg2+ and L-PA was added to the ratiometric fluorescence sensor, because the interaction between L-PA and Hg2+ prevents the combination of sulfhydryl groups and Hg2+ on the surface of CdTe QDs. Thus, ions binding were the main process of CdTe QDs quenching (Chen et al., 2019). The results showed that the competition between Hg2+ and CdTe QDs surface sulfhydryl groups leads to fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of NH2-MIL-101(Fe), CdTe QDs and down/up-conversion ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the stability of NH2-MIL-101(Fe), the NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was kept at room temperature for 60 days and the fluorescence stability was assessed every 5 days. As shown in Fig. S5A and S5B, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) remained stable over 60 days with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of fluorescence intensity change was less than 4.9 %, indicated that the NH2-MIL-101(Fe) possessed good fluorescence stability. To evaluate the stability of CdTe QDs, the CdTe QDs were placed in the dark at room temperature for 60 days and the fluorescence stability was evaluated every 5 days. As illustrated in Fig. S5C and S5D, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs remained unchanged for 60 days with the RSD of fluorescence intensity change was less than 2.7 %, indicating that the prepared CdTe QDs have good fluorescence stability. As shown in Fig. S5E and S5F, the fluorescence intensity of the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor remained stable under continuous irradiation at a 365 nm UV lamp for 30 min, indicating that the sensor had photobleaching resistance. In addition, the stability of the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor was also investigated using a fluorescence spectroscopy system. The down-conversion fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F554) and up-conversion fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F554) of the fluorescence sensor remained stable for 60 days with the RSD of fluorescence intensity change was less than 1.6 % (shown in Fig. S5G and S5H), indicating that the prepared fluorescence sensor has good fluorescence stability. Moreover, NaCl solution with high concentration range (1 mol/L–10 mol/L) was added to the fluorescence sensor to evaluate the effect of high salt environment on the performance of the fluorescence sensor (Liu et al., 2022a, 2022b; Tang et al., 2022a, 2022b, 2022c). As shown in Fig. S5I and S5J, NaCl solution with high concentration showed little of influences on the fluorescence intensity even at the concentration of 10 mol/L. The results indicated the high stability of the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

To evaluate the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor toward Hg2+, the effect of different metal ions on the fluorescence response of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was investigated under the same conditions, including K+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Ni+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cd2+ and Ag+. As shown in Fig. 5A, when Hg2+ was added to the ratiometric fluorescence sensor, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly, while the fluorescence effect of other interfering metal ions on the fluorescence sensor was same as that of the blank sample except for Cu2+ and Ag+. The reason for the quenching of CdTe QDs fluorescence by Hg2+, Cu2+and Ag+ may be that the sulfhydryl groups on the surface of CdTe QDs form HgS, CuS and Ag2S compound with Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+, in which the solubility product constant values (Ksp) of HgS (Ksp = 4 × 10−53), CuS (Ksp = 6 × 10−36) and Ag2S (Ksp = 2 × 10−49) are lower than those of CdS (Ksp = 8 × 10−27), so that both Hg2+, Cu2+and Ag+ can quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs, while HgS has a lower Ksp value than CuS and Ag2S (Labeb et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020b). Thus, Hg2+ has a stronger affinity compared to Cu2+ and Ag+. As shown in Fig. 5B, Cu2+and Ag+ may interfere with the analysis of Hg2+, which was effectively avoided by the addition of sodium diethyl-dithiocarbamate (DDTC) in the determination system (Gong et al., 2016). All the results showed that the sensor has good selectivity for Hg2+.

To evaluate the selectivity of the NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/CdTe QDs/Hg2+ system toward L-PA, the changes in the fluorescence intensity of this system after the addition of others potential coexisting substances were discussed. Fig. 5C shows the chemical structure of L-PA and its analogues. As shown in Fig. 5D, the NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/CdTe QDs/Hg2+ has a better selectivity toward L-PA, while other interfering substances have less effect on the fluorescence sensor. The results indicated the NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/CdTe QDs/Hg2+ system has good selectivity toward L-PA.

The actual monitoring of Hg2+ and L-PA is of great importance for environmental evaluation and human health. Thus, different concentrations of Hg2+ and L-PA added to water, fish, pork and human urine samples were tested using the standard addition method. Table S1 and Table S2 demonstrated the corresponding results of the two analytical methods for the detection of Hg2+ and L-PA concentrations in the practical samples. The recoveries of Hg2+ in lake water, river water, fish, pork and human urine were 98.1–103.9 %, 99.8–105.3 %, 99.5–100.3 %, 99.7–100.2 %, 99.8–100.2 %，respectively. And the recoveries of L-PA in lake water, river water, fish, pork and human urine were 99.4–101.0 %, 100.2–104.0 %, 99.6–101.1 %, 101.1–102.2 %, 97.4–104.4 %, respectively. In addition, the recoveries of Hg2+ and L-PA were 99.2–106.3 % with RSD of 2.5–4.9 % and 99.4–103.4 % with RSD of 2.8–4.7 % based on smartphone, respectively. These results illustrated the practicability and suitability of ratiometric fluorescence analysis with a smartphone-assisted platform for the detection of Hg2+ and L-PA in complex sample systems, offering the possibility of monitoring multiple target analytes for ecological and human health. The properties of the down/up-conversion fluorescence sensors were compared with previous methods for the determination of Hg2+ and L-PA in Tables 1 and 2
. The down/up-conversion ratiometric fluorescence sensors achieved dual-mode fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and L-PA, which improved the experimental accuracy. Compared with other traditional single fluorescence sensors, the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor achieved both visual detection and detection of large anti-stokes shifts (Fu et al., 2022b). Compared with other analytical methods, the smartphone-assisted fluorescence analysis simplified the complex detection process without large equipment. All results proved that the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor is a strong contender for ecological environment and human health monitoring.

In summary, a detection system combining a down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor with a smartphone was proposed to detect Hg2+ and L-PA in different environments. The detection limits of Hg2+ and L-PA in the down-conversion ratiometric fluorescence system were 0.053 nM and 1.10 nM, respectively. The detection limits of Hg2+ and L-PA in the up-conversion ratiometric fluorescence system were 0.105 nM and 2.93 nM, respectively. Compared to previous fluorescence sensors, the down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor has a lower detection limit. In the smartphone system, the two good linear ranges was 0.12 nM–0.92 nM and 0.92 nM–1222 nM with a LOD of 0.091 nM for Hg2+ and 10.0 nM–360 nM and 360 nM–4410 nM with a LOD of 8.97 nM for L-PA. In addition, the concentrations of Hg2+ and L-PA were evaluated by three-dimensional dynamic analysis in complex environments. The smartphone-assisted down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence sensor provides the potential application of food safety, environmental detection, and human health monitoring.

Kangling Tang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing-Original draft. Yu Chen: Resources, Methodology. Sisi Tang: Data Curation. Xiaodan Wu: Formal analysis. Pengfei Zhao: Validation.
